video_title: Lawfare Daily: How To Make a Patriot, with Fabian Hoffman
transcript: It's the lawfare podcast. I'm Anastasia Patina, Ukraine fellow at Lawfair with Fabian Hoffman, a senior research fellow at the program for Ukraine and full spectrum threats at the Norwegian Institute for Defense Studies. Fabian and I discussed whether the US will give a license for the production of Patriot interceptors to Ukraine and what it actually takes to make them. So Fabian uh we invited you back on the podcast to discuss some of the recent news that came out of the uh NATO summit in Ankara in Turkey which happened in the first week of July. At that summit the US President Donald Trump said that the US will be giving a license to Ukraine to produce Patriot interceptors. And he just kind of dropped that in the middle of a of a you know press appearance. And as far as I understand, we don't really have any details of, you know, what that will look like yet. That the the deal, the license hasn't yet been finalized. So, we don't know all that much. But, of course, it spurred, you know, a whole lot of coverage of the issue, especially considering that the air war between Ukraine and Russia is getting worse by the day, very much literally by the day. I'm based in Kiev and u it's an unprecedented level of attacks by ballistic missiles. we, you know, we Ukrainians in Kiev would would get ballistic strikes, you know, two, three, four days in a row, which has never happened before. And at the same time, Ukraine and Ukrainian authorities are saying that uh we've run out of interceptors completely. So, in the context of all of this, of course, we wanted to bring you on to discuss all of this and discuss the Patriots and the air war. So, thank you so much for coming on. And I want to start with asking you a very basic question to just sort of set the set the rules here. Can you explain how does licensing actually work as in who grants the license to whom and what does a license allow you to do? >> Yeah. So basically you have two actors here. One is the licenser, the the manufacturer that crs the license and then have you have the license uh the actor that receives the license and typically the licenser is a foreign manufacturer granting a license to a domestic manufacturer depending of course in which country you're living in. Um but in this case, Rathon, for example, for the PAC 2 GMT interceptor would grant a Ukrainian manufacturer the the license to produce Patriot manufacturer uh sorry, Patriot interceptors inside Ukraine. Now this involves technology transfers. This involves military technology transfers. So governments are also always involved. In the US case, it is the US government that needs to allow, right, the manufacturer to grant the license for these interceptors to be produced outside the the United States. So, this means it's not just a businessto business deal. You always have the official side involved, which is why this hinges on a political agreement to work. >> Okay? So this would need to involve not just the Ukrainian government you know the president whatever you know relevant institution we have but also a specific company. So hypothetically for example firepoint which is you know trying to make ballistic missiles in Ukraine >> could could be but this is already you know a bit where the issue starts that very often or or you know let's take a step back for example when Rathon granted a license to MBDA Germany to produce pack 2 GMT interceptors that was a very logical choice because MBDA Germany has been heavily involved in missile defense production in the past and basically was already part of this defense industrial base involved in in production of these types of weapon systems, right? Not exactly the same, but broadly speaking, there were component manufacturers that could fairly easily be be diverted for for this program. Now, if you look at Ukraine, this is where the first issue starts. I I would say there is not, you know, an immediate Ukrainian manufacturer that comes to mind where you would say, okay, look, this is the logical partner to be the prime contractor under a license production agreement between the United States and and Ukraine. So, it could be Firepoint, it could be someone else, but you know, there is not your one most logical choice. I would say >> we'll get to talking more about this, but before we get specifically into the Ukraine context, can you just explain a little bit more the logic behind the licensing agreements? Like what do both parties get out of, you know, granting these licenses? >> Yeah, I mean in the end it's a it's a business deal. Again, it it involves governments, but it is, I would say, you know, economically beneficial for for both sides outside any military political context. The licenser increases right the production base of its missile system in this case right an interceptor could of course be any other military product by moving production outside existing facilities. So you increase the number of components that are being produced. You increase the number of entire units that are being produced. So in the end you can sell more of course. Then license agreements they also allow you to gain market share. So very often they are used as a tool to to make acquiring a system more attractive for the for the side receiving it. So instead of you know just saying hey look we're going to export the weapon system to you we're going to integrate you in the supply into the supply chain making this whole deal a lot more attractive from the side of the seller and then of course for the licenser it's also just uh you know fees that come with it. So let's say you have established a licensed production manufacturing facility somewhere abroad. Every time that facility builds an interceptor, a missile or whatever product and sells it to another customer, you get a license licensing fee, right? Because it is your intellectual property that is being sold. Hence, you get a share of whatever profit is generated. So in that sense it can also be economically very very beneficial for the licenser. For the lency well they get access to an established product to established IP. That means you can save a lot of the development costs behind you know some of these ballistic missile defense systems for example or again for any other military product. And of course the technology transfers well immediately they are restricted to the product that your license producing. Of course, receiving that technology and bringing it into your own defense industrial base. Well, this creates competencies beyond the very product that you are building in that instance. Right? So in the context of Ukraine again if they were to produce Patriot interceptors themselves yes that technology first is restricted to that interceptor system but the training the facilities the broader logistics competencies knowledges that come with it they can of course be useful beyond that specific program >> and it is also very much a sensitive political decision on the part of the licenser right especially when it comes to systems like patriot which is one of the most valued one of the most important you know weapons in the American arsenal correct me if I'm wrong >> no 100% correct uh this is very very sensitive and this is why you know licensed production agreements they are and they should be seen as much more than just a business deal and and very often in the past they have been used actually to you know deepen political relations to enhance alliance cohesion because they signal trust trust right if I am if I as the government if I'm willing to transfer some sensitive technologies to a country abroad well that means I must trust the country because if if I don't trust the country they can easily divert that technology right they can they they can say look um you know they can take that they can build their own product based on that they can make all sorts of shady things with it if they want to they can sell it further right this is also a huge concern they can let competitors into their facilities and take a look at, you know, what what the product looks like, the specifications and whatnot. So, this is not something you you would simply do without thinking very very hard about it. >> Okay. Now, let's talk a little bit more in depth about the Patriot itself. [snorts] The Patriot system is is very complicated and has many parts to it. Can you describe the various parts of this air defense system and which companies produce what? >> Yeah, so I would say three main components. The one is the the interceptor that is most often talked about right now, right? This is the missile system that is used to intercept the incoming projectile. But of course, for that interceptor to work and be useful, you need a launcher system and a platform from which you can fire that interceptor against the incoming target. Then you need a radar to to locate and track the target. And there's a fourth component or actually four main components which is you know a battle management system which which integrates all these different components that they all work together and Rathon is overall the main contractor for the overall Patriot system. So they produce most of the components. I I would have to look up the details as well, but to my knowledge, the the launcher is built by by by Rathon. The radar definitely comes from Rathon. The battle management system. I'm not sure. I think the Northrop Kraman might also be involved at least for the newer generation one. And then with the interceptors, you have two main interceptors right now for for Patriot. The one is the PAC 2 GHMT, slightly older, slightly less advanced, which is built by Rathon. And then you have the more modern, more advanced PC 3 MSE interceptor, which is built by Loheed Martin. >> Can you describe a little bit more the difference between these two interceptors, the PAC 2 and the PAC 3. >> So the the PAC 3 MSE interceptor, so the most advanced interceptor for Patriot, it uses what we call hit tokill technology. So you have a kinetic kill vehicle which literally is a part of the interceptor that is going to fly directly into the incoming projectile. So you destroy you intercept the the projectile a ballistic missile by flying directly into it. And that is of course pretty difficult, right? People have described it as shooting down a bullet with a bullet. Just that in reality, ballistic missile defense is is more difficult because the projectiles they approach each other with higher velocities sometimes as as bullets from a gun do. And of course, both maneuver, right? So, this is really really tricky. Requires really advanced technology and and this is something that Loheed Martin have have more or less mastered, right? Or at least they're the most advanced company in this space. And if you can master it, if you can build these types of interceptors, they are very effective. Which is why PC 3 MSE is widely regarded as the most effective ballistic missile interceptor, at least for short-range ballistic missiles like the Iscander. And the PAC 2 CHMT. So this is the other interceptor, the other main interceptor used for for Patriot doesn't work via hit tokill technology. Um instead it uses a fragmentation warhead. which you know when it gets close to the target detonates and through the shock wave through the fragments that are dispersed outward destroys the incoming projectile ideally. There are also some differences with the guidance mechanisms but you know those are those are the main main differences. So am I right in understanding that the US is the only country in the world that produces and you know has the right to produce the Patriot launchers and the system and everything that is not the interceptor. So the radar, the you know the control station, the launchers that can only be made in the US, right? Um, as far as I know, yes, I I would have to confirm with with Japan, but I think they also only are allowed to build the the interceptor. Germany for sure, right? Germany has been granted a license by Rathon to build the interceptor, but nothing else surrounding it. So, uh, yes, with with the exception of Japan, which I would have to confirm as well, they are the only producers of those other components other than the the interceptor. Mhm. So, you've already mentioned briefly that Japan and Germany have been given licenses years ago to make interceptors for uh the Patriot air defense system. Can you talk about those deals a little bit because I think they're very illustrative for, you know, the the future of Ukrainian production. >> Yeah. Yeah, of course. So, so Japan has a long history in building Patriot interceptors. So that already started you know before the turn of the the millennia that Japan was building first PAC 2 baseline interceptors. Then they transitioned to PEC 3 baseline interceptors and now in 2025 they have received the first production uh contract for PACF free MSE interceptors in Japan. And so Japan [snorts] is the only country outside the United States which is allowed to license produce the most advanced Patriot interceptor. And production numbers a few years ago they were at around 30 interceptors per year. So really not a lot at all. And it was entirely for the Japanese market. So there was no role at all in exporting these systems. Although there have been then talks about using Japanese production to backfill American arsenal stockpiles that have been sent to Ukraine, but that's it. There has been talk to expand that production capacity potentially to around 100 a year, but from the open sources, it's not entirely clear where where this stands. The other country that is allowed to to produce Patriot interceptors and license is Germany, but not the PAC 3 MSE interceptor. Instead, the PAC 2 CMT interceptor. So basically, Germany received a license from Rathon for their interceptor whereas Japan received a license from Loheed Martin for for their interceptor. and Germany. They have built up a new facility in in suffering Germany with MBDA Germany for the license production of PAC2MT interceptors and they're going to produce potentially around 150 180 interceptors once the facility is is fully up and running and it will start production later this year. So in Germany's case, it it was granted the license in 2022, right? >> No. So the the announcement was made in 2024. So this is when the former process started. We don't know, you know, how how long they've been talking about this behind the curtain, but 2024 is when we receive the the confirmation that this will take place. >> Okay. But Germany is going to need at least two years or it you know by the time it starts actually making them it it's going to take them at least two years to get this up and running. Right. >> Yeah. So the the first announcement came in January 2024. They started building the facility in November that year. So end of 2024. They will start production somewhere towards the end of this year. So around fall winter 2026. And according to reports, the first full units of interceptors will be shipped sometime 2027 and and transferred to the first customer which is the the Bundesear the German army. >> Okay. So now let's talk about the Ukrainian context. First of all, I'm wondering if Ukraine is actually granted a license to build these interceptors. Do we know if this is going to be a license for PC 3 or PE 2? That's a question. >> No, we don't have any details yet. I mean, we, you know, right now this is a a political commitment more or less because it's Trump who >> not even a commitment anyway. Yeah, exactly. That Trump made, >> right? It's a it's an announcement and we know how much of a how how useful commitments by Trump sometimes are. So, we we don't actually know yet if this will take place, right? So, this is now where probably behind the scenes they're trying to figure out what was meant by that. I'm pretty sure there was you know some preparatory work done by the manufacturer because you know they they thought you know something like this might be coming and again they might be also interested it might be an economic interest to to do this so I could imagine that there are some some work that was done in the past on this behind the curtains but yeah now we have to figure out what this looks like we don't know right there was no indication whether it would be pack 3 MSE or pack 2 GMT however I am very very certain that if this license production agreement comes about, it will concern the PAC 2 GMT and not the PAC 3 MSE. So, Ukraine would get a license to build a somewhat less advanced interceptor. >> Why? >> Well, several reasons. I mean, you know, if you if you look at it in in 2024, Ryan Metal, they they indicated very clearly that they want to build packfree MSE interceptors with uh Loit Martin in in Germany in their new missile facility in Unulus. And apparently, and we we don't know, but initially there might have be been an interest from the from the manufacturer to pursue this, but this never really went anywhere. And one likely explanation is that either the the manufacturer they you know they lost interest in this and they decided to concentrate their efforts on their their home market the United States or that the US government was just like never in favor of this type of technology transfer which again might be a very reasonable assumption given how advanced and critical this technology is. But in the end, you know, whatever the reason was, I I cannot imagine that this calculation will fundamentally shift with the context changing. I mean, if you think about, you know, Ukraine, a country in wartime, a country that the United States probably does not trust as much as Germany, why would they receive this technology if Germany wasn't good enough for it? So I would be very very surprised if if if there was a change in in that policy. And the other factor of course is just also technological. Again we briefly touched up on it. Building ballistic missile interceptors >> Champions League of missile technology that's really difficult to do. >> Ukraine does not have a strong defense industrial base for it. So, it might also just be more feasible um much better to just pursue the less advanced option than immediately go for the the high-tech option. >> Right. Okay. The next question I have is if Ukraine was to get the license, does Ukraine have to actually build these interceptors inside Ukraine or can it be outside of Ukraine for security reasons? No, it could of course be outside outside of Ukraine for for security reasons and others in in the end, right? License production, it only makes sense, especially now in the case of of Ukraine. In in peace time context, it might be a bit different, but in the context of Ukraine, license producing these missile systems really only makes sense if you can expand the defense industrial base building these systems, right? If if license production just means we're putting a final assembly facility in Ukraine, then you have gained nothing because all the different components that you need to build, the seeker, the warhead, the propulsion system, the board computer, the battery, right? All of these systems, well, they're still produced in the same number. And then it doesn't really matter if I do the final assembly in Ukraine or in the United States or elsewhere because the overall number will stay the same. So if >> okay so explain this what's what is the right way to do this then? Yeah. So the right way to do this if you seriously want to pursue license production in a way that makes sense you have to grow the the component production base right so instead of just building another final assembly facility that's you know not easy but that's I would say a less hard step I have to make sure that I find new suppliers that can build you know the the guidance suite that can build the warhead that can build the propulsion unit so that I can increase the overall supply base for the system that I'm I'm licensed producing because only that way I can actually expand the overall production numbers and you know that's that's again why it's so important or why it's so beneficial that if I'm doing a licensed production agreement that there is a suitable defense industrial base already in the country because if I have to build that up from scratch well then things will take very very long. >> So is Ukraine going to have to look for all of these suppliers within its borders? >> Um they no need that they have to right they they could and you know it really depends then also on how that license production agreement is negotiated and you know then you really get into the the intricacies of the the contractual work that will be done. I mean it could it could also be more of a panuropean license production agreement where we're saying that you know Ukraine is building maybe or contributing the warhead technology right so they are trying to build up a supply chain for the warhead whereas we try to source the the seeker technology from outside might be from deal defense might be from tales or you know another >> what are all of these names you're you're saying >> so those are other manufacturers Right. So, Deal Defense would be a German missile manufacturer, Tales, a big French eur French manufacturer, base systems, big British manufacturers. So, they could all be integrated into the supply chain. It just, you know, then it also gets more complicated again because you have more borders involved, more legislation, more governments with their own interests. So, but yeah, this is all speculative, right? But this is certainly something you could do theoretically. If you want to speed up things, this might be the the only feasible way. Okay. So, a lot is going to depend on what the actual deal looks like, right? >> And you know, you could then even make the argument, okay, so what's the point in building up, you know, a production line, a supply chain now in in in Ukraine or pan European, you know, then you could also just say, let's invest some money and expand the production facility in Germany that already exists, right? Maybe that's the easier way. And then you just say, look, everything that is coming out of this expanded production facility just goes straight to Ukraine. By the way, a lot of the the production that's going to come out of of the suffery facility is already destined for for Ukraine, at least indirectly. So I guess I mean for Ukraine of course this is about trying to be as self-sufficient as possible but as you as you've described that relies on the supply chain and if Ukraine can't localize as much of that as possible then you know this is a little counterproductive right and yeah so again they're competing interests right so I would say politically economically it of course makes a lot of sense for Ukraine to localize as much as possible not not least you know because if I have a local supply chain. >> All of this is extremely lucrative. >> This is lucrative. Yeah. For for several reasons, right? If I if I have a lot of the added value being produced in Ukraine, uh that's, you know, tax money flowing back to the government when they place orders for these systems, that's that's one benefit. Again, the defense industrial base that you're going to grow in advance, that's another benefit. Perhaps after the war, right? Even if this is a longerterm project, let's say it takes three to four years to build up that supply chain. If you have it in your country, this could be very lucrative h in the future because you know patriot demand is probably not going to decrease very suddenly. So this could be you know useful for export contracts later on. So many reasons why you would want to localize as much as possible of that supply chain. But if it's if it's just about capability right now, right, then well in a short term there's nothing really you can do anyways. But then it might be better to say look we go softer on the localization and instead prioritize um you know moving some of it at least outside to speed up the the whole process. >> And what about the actual engineers working on this? I mean is there some massive gap in just training and qualifications when it comes to this? Like is does Ukraine even have enough brains and people to do this work? >> I I don't think that's the main bottleneck. I think that's that's pretty okay. You know, Ukraine has very talented engineers as in with the rest of Europe. So that's that's totally fine. You know, you can train them, you can teach the processes, you can ship the the machines that you need. That's all fine. >> Mhm. >> The the bigger issue is what we call certification, right? If I let's say I've I want to expand the production base of these interceptors and I want to integrate a new component supplier, I have to certify that supplier to for it to become part of the supply chain and >> certify through whom? >> So certification through the again that could involve different actors but I mean through the manufacturer at home right >> that is a big role. The US government probably wants also some type of certification, some type of reassurance. I mean, you know, this is detailed knowledge which I don't have. I haven't been involved myself with these these agreements, but that's how I imagine it would go, right? Because for example, the US government again, they want reassurance that their technology is not diverted. So part of the certification procedure is for example has nothing to do with building up the technology. It's also just about reassuring the the licensing government that you know the technology is safe. So they want to know hey what are your protocols for making sure that that technology is not stolen. Who do you let into the facility? How do you make sure that Russian spies or or French spies right who might be interested in some American ballistic missile defense technology how that they don't get into the facility? Right? These are fine questions that you are allowed to ask as the licenser and that the lency has to answer. Okay. Another question is when when you get a license to produce a certain type of technology. Am I right in understanding that you're only allowed to replicate that exact technology, you know, sort of word by word as the original model and you're not allowed to sort of put a spin on it, adjust it to your battlefield needs like you know, you know what I like you're not allow you're not allowed to develop it in any way, which is probably what some Ukrainian companies would want to do because as far as I understand, Ukraine has been using Patriots a little bit more creatively and a little bit differently than the Americans and and their allies have traditionally used them. >> Yeah. I mean, they have used them operationally different, right? They as far as we know, they haven't changed any of the the underlying technology. I mean, you know, if you're Ukraine and I would say if you use them domestically and if I Yeah. I mean first of all the the missiles would be built to standard of the manufacturer >> because again you you certify the supply chain to produce this specific type of product. If you change processes in the supply chain that certification lapses and suddenly you're not allowed to produce them anymore. >> Okay. Once the the product is final and assembled and you know shipped to to the end user theoretically they can use that for whatever purpose and they can do whatever they want to with it. So if they would want to make modifications that might not be very well liked by by United States but they could do it theoretically. I I mean it gets it gets more tricky when exports are involved. So let's say a Ukrainian supply chain manufactures something a Patriot interceptor and ships it to an export customer then well the export customer and the United States would expect that this is a system specifically built to the standards of what's coming out of the United States right so then I would say you're you probably can't do it >> okay so considering everything we've just discussed what could be the perspective timeline for this like what are what are you what are your expectations like when is Ukraine going to be able to actually produce its own pet trade interceptors. You probably hate this question, I know, but I got to ask it. >> No, that's fine. I mean, optimistically, 12 to 24 months, realistically more than 24 months, two to three to four years. And you know, again, if you if you would probably if you want would want to localize everything, things take longer. If you're willing to move some component production abroad, involve, you know, for example, German component suppliers and scale them. um maybe things can go go faster, but it's in in any case, it's not a short-term fix, right? This is something that will have effect over the medium to long term. >> Okay. Well, Fabian, as always, it was a pleasure speaking with you. Thank you for coming. >> Yeah, thanks so much for having me. That was great.
